method3.generated_article:
==Flora and fauna==
No source documents were provided, so I cannot write a cited “Flora and fauna” section using the required document-number citation format.

==Economics==
Howland Island has no permanent inhabitants, limiting on-island economic activity to government administration, conservation, and occasional authorized access rather than a resident local economy.[1] Historically, the island’s main exploitable resource was guano: the United States claimed Howland in 1857 under the Guano Islands Act, but the deposits are now exhausted.[1] In the 1930s, the atoll was colonized from Hawaii, placed under the administration of the U.S. Department of the Interior, and used as a planned aviation stop on routes between Hawaii and Australia.[1] Modern economic use is constrained by its status as a U.S. National Wildlife Refuge and as part of the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument.[1] Fisheries activity occurs in the broader U.S. Pacific Remote Island Areas, which include Howland Island, but this region accounted for only a small share of deployments in the referenced Hawaii-based pelagic fishery data.[3]

==Time zone==
Howland Island observes UTC−12:00, the same time zone as nearby Baker Island in the United States Minor Outlying Islands.[1][2] In nautical time-zone notation, this offset is designated zone Y, or “Yankee”.[2] Because the UTC−12:00 zone is on the Western Hemisphere side of the International Date Line, Howland and Baker are the last places on Earth where a calendar date remains valid under the “Anywhere on Earth” convention.[3] Under that convention, a day is considered to end when it ends on Howland Island.[3] The island is uninhabited, and public entry is allowed only by special permit, so the last inhabited places to experience the end of a day are instead generally in UTC−11:00, such as Niue and American Samoa.[3][4][5] The time difference between Howland Island and Kiribati can be 26 hours, reflecting Kiribati’s position on the other side of the International Date Line after its 1995 realignment to keep all of its islands on the same calendar day.[1][5]

==History==
The first recorded sighting of Howland Island was in 1822 by George Worth, a Nantucket whaling-ship captain, who named it Worth Island.[2] In 1842, Captain George E. Netcher of the New Bedford whaleship Isabella named the island Howland, reportedly for the ship’s lookout.[2][4] The United States claimed Howland in 1857 under the Guano Islands Act of 1856, and guano-mining operations followed after competing American companies sought to exploit the deposits.[2][4] A dispute between the United States Guano Company and the American Guano Company was settled in 1865, allowing both companies to mine the island’s guano.[4] These guano-mining enterprises ended in 1878, after the deposits had been depleted.[4]

In 1935, colonists from Hawaii arrived as part of the American Equatorial Islands Colonization Project, also known as the Hui Panalaʻau Project, to establish a permanent United States presence in the central Pacific.[4] The settlement they founded on Howland was named Itascatown, after the U.S. Coast Guard Cutter Itasca.[4] The island was placed under the administration of the U.S. Department of the Interior in 1936.[2] During the 1930s, Howland was developed for use as a stop for aircraft flying between Hawaii and Australia.[2] On July 2, 1937, aviator Amelia Earhart and navigator Frederick J. Noonan disappeared near Howland after departing Lae, Papua New Guinea, during Earhart’s attempted around-the-world flight.[2] During World War II, the island served as a lookout point for the U.S. military.[1] Today, Howland is administered as a U.S. National Wildlife Refuge and was included in the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument in 2009.[2]

==National Wildlife Refuge==
Howland Island National Wildlife Refuge was established in 1974 and originally included the island, surrounding waters, and submerged and emergent lands from the mean low-tide line out to 3 nautical miles.[4] The refuge protects a small coral-fragment island surrounded by an active fringing reef, providing nesting and foraging habitat for seabirds and other marine wildlife.[1] There are no permanent inhabitants, and refuge management planning has proposed no general public uses, limiting activity primarily to authorized conservation, monitoring, and research.[2][5]

After the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument was established in 2009, the refuge boundary around Howland was extended to 12 nautical miles, while the monument encompasses waters and submerged and emergent lands out to 50 nautical miles.[4] The monument area around Howland is cooperatively managed by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration.[4] Its designation is intended to protect and preserve marine environments and associated historic and scientific objects.[4]

Management priorities for the refuge include biological surveys, habitat-condition assessment, seabird conservation projects, marine habitat exploration, and expanded opportunities for scientific research.[5] Planning documents for Howland and related Pacific refuges were prepared under the National Wildlife Refuge System Administration Act, as amended, and the National Environmental Policy Act.[5] Conservation issues identified for the island include past impacts from invasive species and broader threats to coral reef ecosystems from global warming and ocean acidification.[4]

==Earhart Light==
A small lighthouse, later known as Earhart Light, was built on Howland Island in 1937, when the island’s landing strips were being prepared for Amelia Earhart’s planned refueling stop during her attempted round-the-world flight.[3][4] Earhart and navigator Fred Noonan never reached Howland, although radio transmissions from her aircraft were heard on the island.[3] The structure was damaged during World War II, when Japanese aircraft attacked Howland and the lighthouse was strafed and partially destroyed.[4][5] Wartime damage also shattered a plaque honoring Earhart, and the beacon was rendered useless.[2][5] The light was rebuilt in the early 1960s, but it has remained inactive since 1942 and is now treated as an abandoned day beacon rather than an operating lighthouse.[3][4]

==Image gallery==
Available gallery material includes maps of Howland Island and an astronaut photograph of the island taken from the International Space Station.[1][2]  
The ISS image, catalogued as ISS010-E-09287, was acquired on 3 December 2004 during Expedition 10 with a Kodak DCS760C digital camera and an 800-mm lens.[2][3][4]  
The photograph shows the island’s coral-fragment surface and the active fringing reef around it, with white breakers marking the reef line.[2][4]  
NASA notes that high-resolution astronaut photographs of reefs are used to update geographic maps, assess reef-ecosystem health, and calculate bathymetry of surrounding ocean floors.[2][4]  
The image is public-domain NASA material produced through the Johnson Space Center’s Earth-observation photography program.[3][4]
